generations.text:
**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**  
*Written on a stormy night, by the light of a flickering oil lamp, with trembling hands and a heart full of regret.*

---

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a heavy heart, for I know you will not read it. But I must. The wind howls like a wounded beast, the sea roars with fury, and the lighthouse stands alone against the tempest. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, I feel the weight of silence more than ever.

You left, as you always have. Not with a shout, not with a tear, but with a quiet, hollow absence. I remember the day you walked out of our home, the way you looked at me—not with anger, but with something colder: disappointment. I did not understand it then, and I still do not. But I do know this: I would have done anything to keep you.

The lighthouse has been my home for many years now. It was once our home, but you took the warmth with you. I have spent countless nights here, watching the sea, listening to the waves, and wondering if I had failed you. I told myself that time would mend what had been broken, but time has only made the silence grow louder.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The wind screams like a banshee

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long, sorrowful years since we last exchanged words, and though the passage of time has worn away the edges of my memory, it has not dulled the ache of your absence.

The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have watched the skies darken with a foreboding that speaks of the storm to come. It is not merely the elements that unsettle me, but the knowledge that I may not see the dawn. I have spent my life tending to this lighthouse, guiding the lost and the weary, and now, as the tempest approaches, I find myself questioning if I have been a good father, if I have done enough to make you proud.

I know you have chosen your own path, and I have tried, in my own way, to let you go. But I have not forgotten the days when you were a child, running through the dunes with your brother, your laughter echoing across the cliffs. I remember the way your eyes would light up at the sight of the sea, as if it held the promise of something greater than ourselves. And I remember the day you left, with your mother's hand in yours, and how I stood at the door, watching you disappear into the horizon, my heart breaking with every step.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghost of my wife, and the sea raging as though it seeks to swallow the very earth beneath me. The lantern has been lit, as it must be, though I fear the light may not be seen beyond the mist. But I write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who thought himself strong enough to bear the weight of your absence. But the years have worn me thin, like the stone of this lighthouse, worn by time and tide. I have seen the dawn and the dusk, the moon and the stars, and still, I have not seen your face in the dark.

I have not spoken to you because I did not know how. Words, once so plentiful, have grown scarce. I have tried to write, but the ink would not flow, and the paper would crumple as if it too longed to be torn. You were my light, Eleanor, and I have been left in the dark.

The storm is coming. I have seen the clouds gather like a shroud, and the sea has grown restless, as though it knows the hour is near. I have prepared the lantern, for it is my duty, and my life. But I wonder if I shall live to see the dawn. I have not been well, and

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like the devil himself outside these stone walls. The sea roars and the waves crash against the cliff like some vengeful spirit, and I fear I may not see dawn. But if I do, I must speak these words to you—before the storm claims me and before I am forgotten.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not written a single letter. I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even care. But I must try.

You were a child when you left, and I did not understand why. I had no idea then that you were carrying a burden I could not see, nor that the world had changed in ways I could not begin to comprehend. I thought you were running from something, but I was wrong. You were running to something, or perhaps from yourself.

I have not stopped watching the sea, Eleanor. Every night, I stand at the edge of the cliff, the lamp in my hands, the light burning bright against the dark. I have seen the horizon change, the stars shift, and the sky darken with storms that have no end. I have become a part of this lighthouse, as much as the stone and the salt and the wind. I have learned to live with silence, with solitude, with the weight of time.

But tonight... tonight is different. The storm is coming,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast and the sea raging in fury. The lighthouse stands, but I fear it may not stand much longer. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of the weight of your absence pressing upon my soul like the tide upon the rocks. I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, but I wonder now if it is for the sea, or for you.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in her heart and a storm in her step. You did not understand why I could not come. You did not understand that I was bound to this place, that the sea and I were bound together. You thought I had abandoned you. But I did not. I could not.

I have watched the waves for so long, and in them, I have seen your face. I have heard your voice in the wind, in the creak of the tower, in the crash of the surf. I have held your memory like a lantern in the dark, guiding me through the nights when the world seemed to fall apart.

You were not the only one who left. I left too, in my own way. I left the warmth of the hearth, the laughter of a family, the comfort of a home. I left to

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since I last saw you, and though I've kept the lighthouse burning day and night, I've kept my thoughts locked away, too—behind the salt-stained glass and the ever-watchful eye of the sea.

You were just a child when you left, and I did not understand the fire in your eyes. You spoke of a future beyond the cliffs, beyond the waves, beyond the life I had known. I feared you would leave not just the lighthouse, but your father behind. I feared I would never see you again. And so I let you go, not with a blessing, but with a curse of silence.

But now, as the storm gathers strength on the horizon, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The wind howls like a beast, the sea roars like a beast, and I am no longer the man I once was. The years have worn me thin, and the lighthouse, though steadfast, is not a fortress. I do not know if I shall see dawn tomorrow.

I have always believed in the power of the light, Eleanor. That it can guide, protect, and, when all else fails, remind us of what we are meant to be. Perhaps you have found your own light in the world beyond these cliffs. Perhaps you have found your own path. But I wonder—

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls. The sea roars, as if it too is weeping. I’ve spent more than half my life here, watching the waves crash against these rocks, and I’ve never seen a storm like this. It’s not just the wind or the waves—it’s the silence between them, the way the world seems to hold its breath, waiting for something to break.

You were never meant to be here. You were meant for a life of comfort, of books and tea and quiet evenings with your mother. But I had no choice. I had to be here, to keep the light burning, to keep the ships from crashing upon these jagged stones. That is my duty, and I have fulfilled it with unwavering devotion. I have not spoken to you in five years, not because I don’t care, but because I thought it was the only way to protect you from the truth.

You left when you were sixteen, just like your mother did. She said she needed to see the world, to find herself. But I know what really happened. You were angry, Eleanor. Angry at me, at the life I gave you, at the sacrifices I made. You thought I was cruel, that I didn’t love you enough to let you go. But I did love you. I just didn’t know how

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside, as if in mourning for the silence that has stretched between us for five long years. Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before I can say what I have long kept locked within.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms. I have seen the sea rage, the skies darken, and the winds howl like the spirits of the dead. But never before have I felt so alone, not in the sense of being physically isolated, but in the soul. You have been gone from my life, and though I have kept the light burning for the sailors who pass by, I have felt it go out within me.

I do not know if you will ever read this. Perhaps the sea will take it before it reaches you, or perhaps you will not wish to hear from me. But I must write, for I fear I may never have the chance again.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. You had a spirit as fierce as the wind that howls through these cliffs. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes full of fire and sorrow. I did not understand then what you were going through, nor did I know the truth of your pain. I was a man of stone, hardened by

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as though it might break with every breath I take. The sea howls outside like a beast in torment, and the wind whips the sails of my soul with a fury that matches the storm. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I pray that this letter finds you—before it is too late.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last heard your voice. I have not spoken to you, not written to you, not even thought of you. And yet, tonight, as the heavens weep and the earth shudders beneath the weight of the tempest, I find myself haunted by your memory. You were my light, my little star, and now I am left in the dark, with only the flickering lamp of this lighthouse to guide me.

I do not know what drove you away, Eliza. I do not know if it was my fault, or if it was the fault of some cruel fate that twisted the hearts of those we loved. But I have spent these years in silence, as if the very act of speaking would bring you back, or at least bring me some peace.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, the waves crashing against the cliffs with a fury that reminds me of the storm in my heart. I’ve kept this letter tucked away for years, but tonight—tonight, with the storm on the horizon and the light still burning—something within me has cracked open.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of empty chairs at the dinner table, of mornings where I would set the kettle on and wait for your voice to greet me. You were the light in my life, Eleanor, and now it seems I’ve been left in the dark.

I’ve tried to remember the last time we spoke. It was a bitter autumn, the sky heavy with rain, and you stood at the door with a suitcase in your hand. “I need to go,” you said, your voice steady but distant. I tried to reach for you, but you pulled away, and the door closed with a finality that felt like an end. I never asked where you went, nor did I try to find you. I told myself it was for your own good, that you needed space, that I was being a fool to cling to a relationship that had grown cold. But I was wrong. I was selfish.

I’ve spent these years watching the sea, the light, the sky.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you more distant than ever. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I find myself wondering if you remember me at all. I am your father, the lighthouse keeper of this lonely coast, and I write to you now not with the hope of reconciliation, but with the solemnity of one who knows his time may be numbered.

The sea has always been my companion, and the wind has always been my friend. But tonight, the sea is restless, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have seen such storms before, and I have stood firm through them, but I am not as young as I once was. The old bones ache, and the light within me flickers like the flame that guides the ships. I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not written to you for so long because I did not know how to begin. Words, once so simple, have become a burden. But now, as the tempest gathers strength, I find that my heart is heavy with words that I must speak—words of love, of regret, of longing.

I have often wondered what became of you. I heard from your mother that you had gone to London, that you had found your own path, and that you no longer needed a father’s hand to guide you. I did not challenge her, for I had long

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heavy heart that I put pen to paper, for I have not written to you in five long years, and I fear this may be the last. The sea has been restless this evening, and the wind howls like a wounded beast, as though the very heavens are crying out. I do not know if I shall see another dawn.

You were but a child when you left this house, and though I told myself I would not mourn your departure, the years have worn me thin. I have longed for your voice, your laughter, the warmth of your presence. Yet I have said nothing, for I feared that if I spoke, I would only bring more pain.

I have always believed in duty, Eleanor. That is what has bound me to this lonely rock these many years. The lighthouse stands, the beacon burns, and I have kept its light steady through storm and calm, through every tempest that has ever threatened these shores. But now, as I stand upon the cliffs, I wonder if I have been blind to the light that has always shone within me—your light.

I have not forgotten the days we spent together, when you were young and full of dreams. You spoke of becoming a writer, of chasing the stars, of not being bound by the sea as I was. I tried to understand, but I was too wrapped in my own world, too afraid to let you go. I never realized

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn. But if I do, I must speak to you—though I know you may not answer.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and in that time, the world has changed, and so has I. I have spent these years alone, with only the lighthouse, the sea, and the endless night to keep me company. The light burns, as it always has, and I have done my duty, as I always have. But I have not done what I should have done—what I *wanted* to do.

You were young when you left, Eleanor. Too young. I remember the day you walked out of the house, your face set with determination, your eyes full of fire. But I have come to understand that love is not always in the same place, and that sometimes, it must be found elsewhere.

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The storm that now gathers on the horizon is but a shadow of the tempest within me, and I fear it may not be the last I shall witness. Yet I must write, for the wind is howling so fiercely that I cannot trust my voice to carry to the shore, and I must be heard, if only by you.  

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and each day since has felt like a century of grief. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the way your eyes turned from mine as though I were some stranger, a specter of the past. I have not forgotten the pain in your voice when you told me you could not bear to stay. And yet, I have not ceased to think of you, not once.  

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, though it is my duty to keep it burning, but the light in my soul. And for that, I have blamed myself.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken and a heart that has long since learned to keep its sorrows to itself. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside this old tower, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. I have not spoken your name in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a lifetime.

You were a child when you left, and I a man who did not know how to hold you. I thought the sea would be kinder than the silence between us. I thought the light would guide you home, and that the nights would be long enough for me to forget the ache of your absence. But the light has always been my duty, and the sea has always been my companion. You, my daughter, became a ghost in the fog.

You called me cruel, and I let it fester in my chest like a wound that would never heal. I did not mean to be cruel, Eleanor. I only meant to protect you. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, I find myself compelled to write, as if the storm itself is a messenger from the past.

You were once my light, Eleanor. The bright, inquisitive child who would climb the steps of this tower with a lantern in hand, eager to learn the ways of the sea. You were the first to ask me, "Why do we keep the light burning, Papa?" and I would tell you, "Because the sea is blind, and the light is our eyes. It is our promise to those who sail the waters, that we will not let them be lost."

But that was before you left. Before you chose to walk away from the life we had built together. You were seventeen, and I was too proud to say the words that were heavy in my heart. I thought that if I simply kept the light burning, if I kept the lighthouse standing, you would come back. But you did not. You left, and I let you go.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

**April 14th, 1892**  

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern’s light flickers, and I wonder if it will hold through the night. I’ve been keeping the light burning all evening, not for the ships, but for you. I’ve been thinking of you more than I’ve ever thought of anyone in my life, and I don’t know why. Perhaps it’s the storm, or perhaps it’s the weight of these five long years pressing down on me like the tide against these rocks.  

I’ve not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, that day you stormed out with your mother, your face twisted with anger and your eyes full of tears. I remember the sound of your voice, sharp as a blade, shouting that I was a fool, that I’d never understand, that I’d never be enough. You called me a *wretched man* and said you never wanted to see me again. I didn’t argue. I didn’t try to stop you. I simply stood there, watching you go, my heart breaking in pieces I never knew I had.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I sit by the window of the lighthouse, the sea howling like a wounded beast, and I am reminded of the storm that once broke upon our shores, the very one that drove us apart. The wind howls through the cracks in the stone, and the waves crash against the cliff with a fury that echoes the turmoil in my heart. I write this letter not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that, should I not survive this night, it may reach you in time.

Five years have passed since you left our home, and I have been left alone with the sea, the wind, and the silence of a heart that has grown cold. I have watched the tides ebb and flow, the stars blink out one by one, and the lantern light flicker in the dark. I have been a man of duty, a keeper of the light, and yet I have felt the light within me dim.

You were always the light in our home, Eleanor. I recall the days when you would run up the spiral staircase, your laughter ringing through the stone halls, and your eyes would sparkle with the wonder of the world. You were the one who taught me to see the beauty in the storm, to find the grace in the tempest. But that light has faded, and I have been left in the dark.

I do not know what drove you away. Perhaps it was the weight of the world,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that has long since grown cold from the weight of silence. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet the memory of your voice lingers in my mind like the echo of a distant bell. I have kept this letter penned in my journal for many weeks, though I have never dared to send it. But tonight—tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, and the sea howls like a wounded beast—I feel that my time is near, and I must speak before the wind swallows my words.

You were never the daughter I wished for, Eleanor. You were too quick to leave, too eager to chase the gilded dreams of the city while I remained here, bound to this stone tower, to the rhythm of the waves, to the solemn duty of guiding ships through the dark. I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and you, my child, were never content with the quiet life. You called me a "bachelor of the sea," and I suppose, in a way, I was. But I never meant to be a stranger to you.

I know the things I said—how I told you to leave, how I told you I would never understand your choices. I was wrong. I was cruel. I did not know then that the sea would one day take more than

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The winds howl like the cries of the damned, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with a fury I have not seen in years. I write this by the dim light of the lighthouse, the beacon’s flame flickering like a dying soul. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must say these words—before the storm swallows me whole.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of a heart that has grown colder with each passing day. I have tried, I have tried to reach you, to remind you of the bond we once shared, the love that bound us as mother and daughter. But you have turned your back on me, and I have not the strength to call you back. Not yet.

You were always so quick to judge, to believe the worst of me. You thought I was cruel, that I locked you out of my life because I did not love you. But I did love you, Eliza. I loved you with a love that has only grown heavier with time. I have watched the world pass me by, and in its wake, I have only the sea and the stone of this lighthouse to keep me company. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast at my door, the sea raging as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern is dim, the oil low, and the storm is upon us. I have not spoken to you in five years, but tonight, I must.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, but never have I felt so alone. The light has always guided the ships, but it has never guided me. You left, Eleanor, and I have not known how to follow. I thought I could wait for you to return, but the years have passed like the tides, and I have only grown more silent.

I have not written to you, nor have I called. I did not know how. You were a child when you left, and I was a man who believed that love could endure any storm. But love, like the sea, can be cruel. You took your own path, and I was left to guard this lonely rock, to watch the world pass by without you.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the way you used to run to the lighthouse, your small hands clutching my coat as you looked up at the light, so bright and so full of hope. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the world whole. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and yet tonight—tonight, as the storm gathers strength—I find myself compelled to reach out, not with words, but with the truth that has weighed on me like a stone all these years.

You were only ten when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. The sun was setting, casting long shadows across the cobbled path, and you stood at the door, your small hand gripping the edge of the gate as though it might hold you back. You said nothing, only looked at me with those eyes—eyes that were not your own, but the eyes of a child who had already decided to leave. I did not stop you. I did not try. I thought, in that moment, that it was better this way. That you would be better off without me.

And so I let you go.

But I never forgot you. Not once. I have watched the sea from this tower, night after night, and I have wondered if you were out there somewhere, lost in the same waves I have seen for thirty years. I have kept the light burning, not just for the ships that pass, but for the hope that

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since you left this place, and yet the wind still howls through the tower as if it knows what I’ve done. I’ve spent every night since then staring at the sea, wondering if you ever looked out at it and remembered me.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. I can feel it in my bones. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves are rising like the devil himself is trying to swallow the coast whole. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but I’m not sure it will be enough. I’ve been preparing for this for years, but tonight feels different. I’ve been told I’m not supposed to be here anymore. That I should have left when I had the chance. That I should have let you go.

I know you think I abandoned you. That I chose the sea over you. But I never chose the sea. I chose this life because it was all I had. I chose it because I thought it would keep me from drowning in the guilt of what I could not give you. I thought I could be a father to you from a distance. I thought I could protect you without being there.

But I was wrong.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for I know not whether the ink upon this page shall ever reach you. The storm brews beyond the cliffs, dark and fierce, as though the very heavens weep for me. I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of longing, of nights spent staring into the fire, wondering if you had forgotten me, or if you had never truly loved me at all. You were once my greatest pride, my light in the darkness. Now, you are a shadow in my memory, a ghost I cannot quite shake.

I have kept this letter for some time, tucked away in the hollow of the lighthouse’s old chest, alongside the old photographs and the faded letters we once exchanged. I had thought, perhaps, that time would soften the edges of our parting. But time, I have come to learn, is a cruel and indifferent thing.

You were so angry, Eleanor. I remember the day you came to the lighthouse, your eyes like flint, your voice like winter wind. You told me you would never return. That you could not live in the same house, the same town, the same world as a man who had never truly seen you. You were right, I suppose. I was blind, and you were the light I could not bear to look upon.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of those years more than ever.

I have always been a man of duty, you know. The light must shine, no matter the cost. And so I have stood here, night after night, through snow and sleet, through storms that would have broken lesser men. But tonight, I fear the storm may be greater than any I've known. The sea is rising, the waves are crashing against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost… personal. I do not know if I will live to see dawn.

I have not written to you in five years, Eliza, and I do not know if I should now. But the wind is howling in my ears, and the sea is whispering of endings, and I feel the need to speak before I am silenced.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, since the day you walked out of our home like a ghost, your eyes filled with fire and your heart with something I could not name. You said you were leaving to find yourself, to find a life beyond this bleak coast, beyond the weight of our past. I did not stop you. I did not try. I thought, perhaps, that it was for the best. That you would find peace where I could not give it.

But now, as the sky darkens and the waves rise like the wrath of the gods, I find myself wondering—what if I was wrong? What if I was too afraid to reach out, too proud to ask for your return? **April 12th, 1895**  
*The Lighthouse, Northumberland Coast*  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea roars with a fury that makes me fear I may not live to see the dawn. Yet, in this moment of peril, I find myself thinking of you—of our last parting, and of the silence that has stretched between us like the wide, uncharted sea.  

Five years, Eliza. Five long years since you left this house, that once was our home, and left me behind. I have not spoken to you in that time, not once, not even in the letters you sent, which I have kept, folded in the drawer beneath my bed, though I have not opened them. I have not opened them. For fear, perhaps, of what they might contain.  

I have not spoken to you in five years. And now, with the storm upon us, I find myself wondering—what if I do not survive this night? What if I am taken by the sea, as I once feared would happen to you? What then? What then would you do? Would you come back, as you once promised? Or would you have forgotten me, as I have forgotten you?  

I have not forgotten you, Eliza. I have not. I think of you every night, when the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening by the light of the flickering lantern, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea is restless, and the sky is heavy with the promise of a storm that will not relent. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again. But tonight, as the world around me seems to tremble on the edge of chaos, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, through every winter's chill and every summer's heat, through every storm that threatened to swallow this island whole. And yet, I have not kept the light of your presence in my heart. I have not spoken to you, not even once. I have not written, not even once. And for that, I am ashamed.

I know you have grown, that you have moved on, perhaps even forgotten me. I know the world has moved on without me, and that you have found your own path, your own life, your own family. I do not blame you. You were only a child when I last saw you, and I was a man who believed that duty was more important than love. I told myself that the sea was my wife, that the light was my child, and that you were only a fleeting shadow in my life.
